reason: The Hebrew people (Israelites) are portrayed very favorably throughout this passage. They are depicted as obedient to God's commands ('all the children of Israel did as the Lord commanded Moses and Aaron, so they did'), recipients of divine favor ('the Lord gave the people favor in the eyes of the Egyptians'), and objects of God's direct intervention and protection. The narrative emphasizes their successful departure from Egypt with their possessions, their orderly organization ('six hundred thousand men on foot, besides women and children'), and their faithful observance of religious ceremonies. The passage frames their exodus as a miraculous deliverance from bondage, with God actively working on their behalf. No negative characteristics or failings are attributed to them in this fragment.
